title: Making the Case to the CFO
scenario: You are the OT Security Manager at Vantara Energy Group, Singapore. You have identified clear gaps in your OT security posture and want to deploy SecureOT Platform. Your CFO, Mr. Raymond Chng, asks: 'I need a business case. Give me one number — what does a successful OT cyber attack actually cost us?'
question: Which argument is most effective when building a financial case for OT security investment with a CFO?
hint: CFOs respond to quantified, business-language risk. Think about production downtime costs, not technical threat descriptions.
opts: Explain that OT cyber risk cannot be quantified in financial terms — present a qualitative risk heat map instead

Quantify the cost of a production stoppage per hour, multiply by realistic recovery time for your sector, add regulatory penalty exposure and reputational risk — then compare that to the annual cost of SecureOT Platform
Tell the CFO that OT security is a compliance requirement and the investment is non-negotiable regardless of ROI
Focus entirely on the technical sophistication of recent OT threat actors — detailed threat intelligence will create the urgency needed
debrief:
Why B: CFOs speak in dollars and probability. A petrochemical plant losing $400,000/hour during unplanned downtime makes the cost of SecureOT Platform an obvious investment. Quantify: production loss per hour × realistic MTTR + regulatory exposure + reputational cost. SecureOT Platform's risk scoring gives you the data to build this case.

🔗 SecureOT Platform capability: Risk scoring + compliance reporting — board-ready risk narrative.

title: The Operations Manager Pushback
scenario: You are Head of OT Security at Hanjin Precision Manufacturing, Ulsan. You are proposing to deploy SecureOT Platform across the production floor. Your Operations Manager, Mr. Baek Seung-woo, says: 'I don't want any security software touching my production systems. Last time IT ran something on the network, we had three hours of unexplained slowdowns. I can't risk that.'
question: How do you address Mr. Baek's concern and get his buy-in for the SecureOT Platform deployment?
hint: His concern is about production disruption — and it's a legitimate one. The answer must directly address what SecureOT Platform does NOT do to the network.
opts: Tell him security is a company-wide mandate and his production concerns are outweighed by the cyber risk — escalate to the CEO if he won't cooperate
Agree to run the deployment only during a planned maintenance window, scanning one subnet at a time to minimise any risk of disruption
SecureOT Platform uses passive, OEM-safe software sensors that never send a single active packet to production devices — it listens to existing traffic only. His systems will not know it is there.
Offer to run a 30-day pilot on a test network first, then present the findings before touching any production systems
debrief:
Why C: Mr. Baek's concern is legitimate and common. The single most important thing to tell him is what SecureOT Platform does NOT do: it does not send packets, does not probe devices, does not touch production systems. It observes existing network traffic passively. No PLC will ever receive an unsolicited packet.

🔗 SecureOT Platform capability: Passive OEM-safe discovery — zero production risk.

title: You Don't Know What's on Your Network
scenario: You are the OT Security Lead at Meranti Pulp & Paper, Riau. Your CISO asks for a full OT asset inventory ahead of an insurance renewal audit. When you pull your existing documentation, you find it was last updated three years ago and the network has grown significantly since then. You have 48 hours.
question: What is the fastest, safest way to produce an accurate, up-to-date OT asset inventory across live 24/7 production subnets?
hint: The method must work on live production systems without any risk of disruption, and deliver results within hours — not days.
opts: Manually walk the production floor with a spreadsheet and physically check each device — most accurate approach with no network risk
Run Nmap in 'safe' mode with low scan rates overnight — modern versions are unlikely to cause issues even on OT hardware

Deploy SecureOT Platform's passive software sensors across the subnets — they will discover every device automatically within hours by listening to existing traffic, with zero disruption to live production
Request the OEM service logs from each vendor — they will have accurate records of every device they have supplied
debrief:
Why C: Manual floor walks miss networked devices. Nmap — even 'safe' mode — sends active packets that can crash legacy PLCs. OEM logs are incomplete and outdated. SecureOT Platform's passive sensors deploy in hours and discover every device that communicates on the network, including devices the OEM has no record of.

🔗 SecureOT Platform capability: Rapid passive asset discovery — complete inventory in hours.

title: The Ransomware Near-Miss
scenario: You are Head of IT/OT Security at Pacifica Steel Works, Newcastle. Ransomware hit your corporate IT network last Tuesday. Your IT team contained it — but only after it had reached the IT/OT network boundary. Your production systems were not affected this time. Your CEO says: 'We got lucky. I want to know: if it had crossed into OT, would we have known in time to stop it?'
question: What is the honest answer — and what does SecureOT Platform's continuous monitoring provide that would have changed the outcome?
hint: Think about what SecureOT Platform watches on the OT side of the boundary — and how quickly a configuration deviation from an intruder would be flagged.
opts: Without SecureOT Platform deployed, the honest answer is no — changes inside the OT network would only be visible when they caused a production fault
Your OT firewall logs would have shown the lateral movement — a SIEM review would have caught it within 24 hours of the crossing
If the ransomware had crossed, your operators would have noticed unusual SCADA behaviour within minutes through normal process monitoring
A network intrusion detection system at the IT/OT boundary would have blocked the crossing before any OT device was reached
debrief:
Why A: Be honest with the CEO. Without OT-side monitoring, configuration changes inside the OT network are invisible until they cause operational impact — by which point you are already in an incident. SecureOT Platform continuously baselines every OT endpoint. Any deviation — a new process, a changed parameter, an unexpected user account — triggers an immediate alert, typically hours or days before production impact.

🔗 SecureOT Platform capability: Continuous baseline monitoring — early detection before operational impact.

title: Selecting the Right OT Security Vendor
scenario: You are the OT Cybersecurity Manager at Meridian Water Authority, Melbourne. You are running a formal vendor selection for an OT security platform. You have shortlisted three vendors. Your environment is predominantly Rockwell Automation. A colleague asks: 'Why would SecureOT Platform be better for us than a vendor-neutral third party? They all say they support Rockwell Automation.'
question: What is the key differentiator that makes SecureOT Platform uniquely suited to a Rockwell Automation-heavy OT environment?
hint: Think about what 'OEM-native' means in practice — what does Rockwell Automation know about its own devices that a third-party vendor cannot?
opts: SecureOT Platform is cheaper than third-party alternatives when deployed on Rockwell Automation hardware due to native licensing integration
Third-party vendors provide broader protocol support across all vendor platforms — SecureOT Platform is only useful if your environment is 100% Rockwell Automation

SecureOT Platform has native, deep protocol knowledge of Allen-Bradley and Logix devices — it can read firmware parameters and configuration states that third-party vendors cannot access, plus closed-loop remediation stays within one platform
All major OT security platforms provide equivalent depth on Rockwell Automation hardware — the real differentiator is SOC analyst headcount and response SLAs
debrief:
Why C: Third-party vendors infer Rockwell Automation device states through passive observation. SecureOT Platform has OEM-native access — direct protocol knowledge of Allen-Bradley and Logix systems, including firmware-level parameters external tools cannot read. In a Rockwell Automation-heavy environment, this depth of visibility is a decisive advantage — and remediation stays within one platform.

🔗 SecureOT Platform capability: OEM-native Rockwell Automation protocol depth.

title: Preparing for an Insurance Audit
scenario: You are the OT Security Manager at Vantara Energy Group. Your cyber insurance provider has sent a 47-question OT security questionnaire as part of renewal. They want evidence of asset inventory, vulnerability management, and monitoring controls aligned to a recognised framework. You have SecureOT Platform deployed.
question: Which of SecureOT Platform's reporting capabilities most directly addresses what a cyber insurer needs to see?
hint: Insurers want evidence of structured, ongoing controls — not a one-time snapshot. Think about which frameworks they recognise.
opts: Export raw asset inventory data from SecureOT Platform and attach it as a spreadsheet — this provides the most complete evidence of visibility

SecureOT Platform's automated compliance reporting generates evidence aligned to IEC 62443, NIST CSF, and NIS2 — giving the insurer structured, framework-referenced documentation of your OT security controls in a format they recognise
Commission an independent penetration test — insurers trust third-party evidence over vendor-generated reports
Prepare a written narrative summarising your security programme — insurers prefer qualitative context over automated tool outputs
debrief:
Why B: Insurance auditors work to recognised frameworks. SecureOT Platform's automated compliance reports map your controls directly to IEC 62443, NIST CSF, and NIS2 — the frameworks insurers and auditors use as reference. A raw spreadsheet doesn't answer their structured questions. Framework-aligned evidence does.

🔗 SecureOT Platform capability: Automated compliance reporting — IEC 62443 · NIST CSF · NIS2.

title: The Unplanned Stoppage Debrief
scenario: You are OT Security Lead at Takamatsu Chemicals, Osaka. A batch process reactor stopped unexpectedly last night — 11 hours of lost production. The vendor found a configuration parameter had changed on a controller. No one admits to making the change. Your plant director asks: 'How do we prove whether this was a cyber event or a human error — and how do we prevent this from happening again?'
question: What does SecureOT Platform's baseline monitoring capability provide that directly answers the plant director's question?
hint: Think about the difference between knowing a change happened and knowing who made it, when, and what the device state was before and after.
opts: SecureOT Platform cannot determine whether a configuration change was made by a human or a cyber attacker — that requires a full forensic investigation

SecureOT Platform timestamps and records every configuration change on every monitored OT endpoint — you can see exactly what changed, when it changed, and what the baseline state was before. This is the forensic evidence for root cause.
The only reliable way to investigate an unexplained configuration change is to interview all engineers who had access to the controller that day
A network traffic capture from the time of the incident would show whether the change was made via the engineering workstation or remotely
debrief:
Why B: SecureOT Platform's continuous baseline monitoring creates a timestamped record of every configuration change on every OT endpoint. You can see: what the parameter was before the change, what it is now, and exactly when it changed. This is the evidence base for root cause analysis — whether human error, authorised maintenance without a change ticket, or a cyber event.

🔗 SecureOT Platform capability: Continuous config baseline — forensic change history.

title: The Board Wants a 3-Year Roadmap
scenario: You are the CISO at Kandalama Industrial Holdings, Colombo. Your board has approved an OT cybersecurity budget but wants a structured 3-year programme with measurable milestones before releasing funds. You have shortlisted Rockwell Automation as your partner. Your board chair asks: 'How do we know this programme will actually improve our security posture — and how will we measure that?'
question: What does Rockwell Automation's Strategic Advisory service produce that directly answers what the board needs?
hint: Boards need measurable milestones, not a product pitch. Think about what a structured assessment and roadmap deliver.
opts: Deploy SecureOT Platform first — the risk scoring data it generates will give the board the metrics they need to define milestones retrospectively
Engage an independent Big 4 consulting firm for the strategic planning phase — external advisors carry more board credibility than a vendor-produced roadmap

Rockwell Automation's Strategic Advisory service starts with a quantified OT risk assessment against IEC 62443 or NIST CSF, then builds a phased roadmap with measurable security maturity milestones — this is the deliverable the board needs before any deployment begins
Present the board with a capabilities overview of SecureOT Suite — the comprehensiveness of the platform is itself the evidence of a structured programme
debrief:
Why C: Boards need strategy before spend. Rockwell Automation's Strategic Advisory service quantifies current OT risk, benchmarks against IEC 62443 or NIST CSF, identifies priority gaps, and builds a phased roadmap with measurable milestones. The board gets exactly what they asked for: a structured plan with clear evidence of improvement over time.

🔗 SecureOT Suite capability: Strategic Advisory — Assess → Design → Implement → Manage.

title: Your IT Team Wants to Own OT Security
scenario: You are the OT Security Manager at Nile Agri Processing, Cairo. Your Group IT Security Director, Mr. Ahmed Mansour, insists that the company's existing IT security programme — which includes a SIEM, endpoint protection, and a 24/7 SOC — is sufficient to extend to the OT environment. He wants to block your SecureOT Platform budget request.
question: What is the most technically accurate argument for why the existing IT security stack cannot adequately cover OT environments?
hint: The gap isn't about the tools being bad — it's about what OT environments require that IT tools were never designed to provide.
opts: IT security tools can adequately cover OT environments — the main barrier is configuration and tuning, which can be addressed through a joint IT/OT project

IT tools use active scanning methods that can crash OT devices, cannot interpret OT-specific protocols, and generate alerts that IT SOC analysts lack the industrial process knowledge to triage correctly — OT requires purpose-built tooling and OT-specialist analysts
The main argument is cost — extending IT tools to OT is more expensive than deploying a purpose-built OT platform
IT security tools work correctly on OT engineering workstations and HMIs — the gap is only at the PLC and sensor level
debrief:
Why B: Three distinct gaps: (1) IT active scanning tools can physically crash OT devices — passive-only is required. (2) IT tools cannot natively decode OT protocols like EtherNet/IP or Modbus — they miss the most important OT telemetry. (3) IT SOC analysts don't have the industrial process knowledge to distinguish a genuine OT threat from a normal process variation. All three require OT-purpose-built solutions.

🔗 SecureOT Platform capability: Passive OT-native monitoring + OT-specialist SOC.

title: A Vendor Update Caused the Incident
scenario: You are Head of OT Security at Solaris Semiconductor, Hsinchu. A firmware update from a trusted OEM vendor caused unexpected behaviour in 14 controllers last month — the update package was later found to contain a malicious module. Your CEO demands to know: 'How do we stop a vendor update from doing this to us again?'
question: What does SecureOT Platform's continuous baseline monitoring provide as a defence against supply chain compromise via vendor firmware updates?
hint: SecureOT Platform cannot block a compromised update — but it can detect what the update did to the device. Think about the difference between prevention and detection.
opts: SecureOT Platform validates firmware packages cryptographically before they are applied — preventing tampered updates from reaching production devices

After any firmware update, SecureOT Platform's continuous baseline monitoring immediately detects any deviation from expected device behaviour — changed parameters, new network activity, or altered processes trigger an alert before the compromise can spread laterally
The only effective defence against supply chain firmware attacks is to air-gap the engineering workstations used to apply updates from both IT and OT networks
Implement a mandatory 30-day hold on all vendor firmware updates before deployment — this gives CISA time to publish advisories if the update is malicious
debrief:
Why B: SecureOT Platform cannot cryptographically verify firmware packages — that is a vendor responsibility. What it can do is detect the effect of a compromised update immediately: any deviation from the established device baseline triggers an alert. The malicious module changed device behaviour. SecureOT Platform would have flagged it within minutes of the update being applied.

🔗 SecureOT Platform capability: Continuous baseline monitoring — detects supply chain compromise impact.

title: Contractor Access Created the Breach
scenario: You are OT Security Manager at Binalong Resources, Perth. A security review has revealed that a maintenance contractor who serviced your conveyor systems eight months ago still has active remote access credentials. No one revoked them. You raise this with your site manager, who asks: 'How common is this problem — and how do we make sure we catch it going forward?'
question: What is the most effective way to continuously monitor for and detect unauthorised or stale access credentials and remote sessions in an OT environment?
hint: Manual credential reviews happen infrequently and miss changes that occur between review cycles. Think about what continuous monitoring would catch in real time.
opts: Conduct a quarterly manual review of all OT system user accounts and remote access sessions — this is the industry standard approach
Implement a strict contractor access policy requiring sign-off before any access is granted — process controls eliminate the risk of stale credentials

SecureOT Platform's continuous endpoint monitoring detects new user accounts and active remote sessions that deviate from the established baseline — any stale or unexpected credential activity triggers an immediate alert rather than waiting for a quarterly review
Require all contractors to use a jump server with session recording — this creates an audit trail even if credentials are not revoked promptly
debrief:
Why C: Quarterly reviews miss the eight months this contractor had active access. SecureOT Platform monitors every OT endpoint continuously — new user accounts, active sessions, and access patterns outside the established baseline all trigger alerts immediately. You catch the stale credential within hours of the maintenance visit, not eight months later.

🔗 SecureOT Platform capability: Continuous endpoint monitoring — user account and access deviation detection.

title: Your OT Team Is Drowning in Alerts
scenario: You are Head of OT Security at Pacific Grid Solutions, Auckland. You have SecureOT Platform deployed and it is surfacing valuable data — but your two-person OT security team cannot keep up with the volume of findings. High-priority alerts are going uninvestigated for days. Your CISO says: 'If we're not actioning the alerts, what's the point of the platform?'
question: What is the right solution to the alert backlog problem — and what does SecureOT Managed Services provide that your two-person team cannot?
hint: The problem is not the platform — it is OT security expertise and capacity. Think about what a dedicated Rockwell Automation managed service team brings.
opts: Reduce the platform's sensitivity settings to lower alert volume — a more targeted alert set is better than an overwhelming one
Hire two additional OT security analysts — the right long-term solution is building internal capacity rather than outsourcing

SecureOT Managed Services provides dedicated Rockwell Automation OT security professionals who monitor, triage, and action SecureOT Platform findings — effectively extending your team with OT-specialist expertise on demand
Implement a tiered alert policy where only Critical and High severity findings are escalated to the security team — Medium and Low findings can be reviewed monthly
debrief:
Why C: Tuning down sensitivity creates blind spots. Hiring takes months and the OT skills market is tight. Ignoring lower-severity alerts creates a backlog of unresolved risk. SecureOT Managed Services puts Rockwell Automation's dedicated OT security professionals to work on your platform — triaging findings, maintaining the asset inventory evergreen, and owning the ongoing security roadmap alongside your team.

🔗 SecureOT Suite capability: Managed Services — dedicated Rockwell Automation OT security professionals.

title: Proving Compliance to a Regulator
scenario: You are OT Security Lead at Nordvik Power Generation, Oslo. Your national energy regulator has given you 60 days to demonstrate IEC 62443 compliance across your OT environment — or face operational restrictions. Your compliance team has been manually gathering evidence for three weeks and has covered less than 20% of the required controls.
question: How does SecureOT Platform's automated compliance reporting capability change the timeline and effort required to demonstrate IEC 62443 alignment?
hint: Think about the difference between manually gathering evidence against each IEC 62443 control versus having a platform that continuously maps your OT environment to the framework automatically.
opts: SecureOT Platform partially automates IEC 62443 evidence gathering — you still need a compliance consultant to interpret the output and map it to specific control requirements

SecureOT Platform generates automated compliance reports natively aligned to IEC 62443 — your asset inventory, vulnerability posture, and configuration monitoring data are continuously mapped to framework controls, producing audit-ready evidence without manual evidence gathering
IEC 62443 compliance cannot be automated — the standard requires human judgement for each control assessment and a signed-off attestation from a qualified assessor
Deploy SecureOT Platform to gather data first, then engage a Rockwell Automation professional services consultant to map the output to IEC 62443 controls in a second engagement
debrief:
Why B: SecureOT Platform's compliance reporting is natively aligned to IEC 62443. Your asset inventory, CVE mapping, configuration baseline monitoring, and access controls data are continuously mapped to IEC 62443 security levels — producing live, audit-ready evidence. What your team has been doing manually for three weeks, SecureOT Platform does continuously.

🔗 SecureOT Platform capability: Automated IEC 62443 compliance reporting — native, continuous.

title: The Legacy Environment Problem
contact: You — Head of OT Infrastructure Security
scenario: You are Head of OT Infrastructure Security at Longhua Steel Industries, Shanghai. Your rolling mill has 340 OT endpoints. At least 60% run Windows XP or Windows 7 — end of life, unpatched, and OEM-certified processes mean you cannot upgrade without an 18-month recertification. Your CISO asks: 'If we can't patch them, what's the point of knowing which CVEs affect them?'
question: How do you explain the value of SecureOT Platform's CVE mapping and risk scoring in an environment where patching is not possible?
hint: Knowing which assets are vulnerable, how critical they are, and what compensating controls exist has value even when you cannot apply a patch.
opts: Agree with the CISO — CVE mapping has limited value if you cannot patch. Focus the investment on network segmentation instead.

SecureOT Platform maps CVEs to each unpatched asset, scores risk by criticality and process exposure, identifies available compensating controls, and monitors continuously for signs of exploitation — giving you a defensible, risk-prioritised security posture even when patching is not an option
Apply for a regulatory waiver to allow emergency patching of the most critical CVEs — the 18-month recertification timeline is a legacy rule that can be challenged
Focus CVE management only on devices at the OT/IT boundary — interior OT devices without internet connectivity carry minimal CVE risk
debrief:
Why B: Knowing you cannot patch does not mean knowing your risk profile is worthless. SecureOT Platform tells you: which of your 340 assets have critical CVEs, which ones are most exposed by network position and process criticality, what compensating controls can reduce exposure, and whether any of those assets show signs of active exploitation. That is the difference between a defensible posture and flying blind.

🔗 SecureOT Platform capability: CVE mapping + multi-dimensional risk scoring + compensating controls.

title: The Midnight Call
scenario: You are the CISO at Tambang Mulia Resources, Jakarta. A critical Rockwell Automation firmware CVE dropped at 11:45pm on a Friday. You have two OT security staff — both on leave. You have 47 PLCs in your processing plant and no quick way to know which are affected. By 3AM you have manually confirmed 8 out of 47. You have a board call at 7AM.
question: What should you have had in place — and what specifically would SecureOT Platform have done differently at 11:45pm?
hint: Think about two things: what the platform already knows about your asset patch state, and who answers the phone at 11:45pm with Managed Services active.
opts: You should have had a more robust manual process — a regularly updated spreadsheet of firmware versions per PLC would have made the Friday night check faster

SecureOT Platform continuously tracks firmware versions and patch status across all 47 PLCs — that query takes 60 seconds on a dashboard. With Managed Services, Rockwell Automation's on-call OT team takes the 11:45pm call so your staff don't have to.
A vulnerability scanner configured to run nightly would have given you an up-to-date patch status report available by morning
The right approach is to have a pre-approved emergency patching window available for critical CVEs — this eliminates the need to check asset status because all patches are applied within 4 hours
debrief:
Why B: SecureOT Platform tracks the firmware version and patch state of every enrolled asset continuously. When the CVE drops, 47 PLCs → one dashboard query → 60 seconds. With Managed Services active, Rockwell Automation's dedicated OT team is already working the incident while your staff are on leave. You walk into the 7AM board call with answers, not estimates.

🔗 SecureOT Suite capability: Continuous asset patch tracking + Managed Services 24/7 on-call response.

title: Scoping an OT Security Programme
scenario: You are the CISO at Hanaro Energy Holdings, Seoul. Your board has approved OT security investment but given no guidance on scope, priority, or timeline. You have five sites across three countries with different OT environments. You don't know where to start. A colleague says: 'Just buy the platform and deploy it everywhere — the data will tell you what to fix.'
question: Why is 'deploy the platform everywhere and see what the data says' the wrong starting point — and what should come first?
hint: Think about what a risk assessment and strategic roadmap give you that raw platform data alone cannot.
opts: Your colleague is right — deploying SecureOT Platform first gives you the asset and vulnerability data you need to build an evidence-based priority list

A risk assessment against IEC 62443 or NIST CSF should come first — it establishes your current security maturity, identifies where the highest risks are across your five sites, and produces a prioritised roadmap before any deployment budget is committed
Start with the site that has the oldest OT equipment — legacy environments carry the highest CVE density and are always the right first deployment target
Submit a full competitive RFP to three OT security vendors and use the evaluation process to define your programme scope
debrief:
Why B: Deploying a platform without a risk-based scope means spending money in the wrong places. A Rockwell Automation Strategic Advisory risk assessment gives you: current maturity level at each site, the highest-risk gaps across all five, a prioritised deployment sequence, and a measurable roadmap. You deploy SecureOT Platform where and how the assessment says — not everywhere at once.

🔗 SecureOT Suite capability: Strategic Advisory — risk assessment and programme roadmap before deployment.

title: The Air Gap That Wasn't
scenario: You are Plant OT Security Manager at Peninsular Chemical Works, Kertih. After deploying SecureOT Platform, the first asset discovery run finds 23 devices your team did not know existed — including an undocumented wireless access point with an active internet-connected SIM card. Your plant director had been operating under the assumption the network was fully air-gapped.
question: What does this discovery tell you about air-gapped OT environments — and how do you brief the plant director on what to do next?
hint: The undocumented WAP is the most urgent finding. Think about what 'air-gapped' actually means versus what organisations discover when they look properly.
opts: Brief the plant director that the air gap is compromised and the facility should be shut down immediately pending a full security investigation

The discovery confirms the air gap was an assumption, not a verified fact. Brief the plant director: immediately isolate the undocumented WAP, verify all 23 unknown devices, and reclassify the network risk posture — this is now an internet-connected environment that requires active monitoring.
Document the 23 devices and the WAP, schedule a remediation project for next quarter, and continue operating under current protocols until then
Escalate to the national cybersecurity authority — an undocumented internet-connected device in a chemical plant OT network is a reportable incident under critical infrastructure regulations
debrief:
Why B: An air gap is only as good as your last complete inventory check. This discovery shows the plant director's assumption was wrong — the network has an active internet path through an undocumented WAP. Immediate actions: isolate the WAP, investigate all 23 unknown devices, reframe the risk posture as internet-connected and deploy continuous monitoring immediately. SecureOT Platform found what years of assumed air-gap security missed.

🔗 SecureOT Platform capability: Comprehensive asset discovery — finds what you didn't know was there.

title: Justifying OT-Specific Incident Response
scenario: You are Head of IT/OT Security at Surin Industrial Coatings, Bangkok. You want to establish a retainer with Rockwell Automation's OT Incident Response team. Your Group IT Security Director says: 'We already have an IT incident response retainer with a global cybersecurity firm. Just extend it to cover OT — it's the same thing.'
question: What is the critical difference between IT incident response and OT incident response that justifies a separate OT-specific retainer?
hint: Think about what OT incident responders must understand that IT responders typically don't — and what happens if containment actions are wrong in an OT environment.
opts: The main difference is documentation — OT incident response requires more detailed chain-of-custody records due to potential regulatory implications

OT incident response requires specialists who understand industrial processes — a containment action that isolates the wrong controller can trigger a safety event or production shutdown more costly than the original incident. Pre-established OT playbooks and process knowledge are not optional.
An IT incident response retainer can adequately cover OT environments if the firm has industrial sector experience — the technical methodology is the same
OT incident response is only meaningfully different for environments with safety instrumented systems — standard manufacturing does not require OT-specific responders
debrief:
Why B: An IT responder isolating the wrong controller in a coating line process can trigger a chemical safety event. OT incident response requires responders who know which systems can be taken offline safely, in what sequence, and what the process impact will be. That knowledge must exist in pre-built playbooks before the incident — not improvised during it. Rockwell Automation's OT incident response team brings both the playbooks and the industrial process context.

🔗 SecureOT Suite capability: OT-specific Incident Response & Recovery retainer.

title: Network Design Before Deployment
scenario: You are OT Security Architect at Nusagraha Pulp & Paper, Riau. You are commissioning a new paper mill and have the rare opportunity to design the OT network from scratch with security built in. Your IT team proposes using their existing flat network model for the production floor — it's simpler and they know how to manage it.
question: Why is a flat network architecture inappropriate for an OT environment — and what architectural principle should govern the design?
hint: Think about what lateral movement looks like in a flat OT network when one device is compromised — and what IEC 62443 says about this.
opts: A flat network is acceptable for a new build if you deploy a next-generation firewall at the IT/OT perimeter — internal segmentation adds management complexity without proportionate security benefit

OT networks must be segmented into security zones by criticality and function with strictly controlled conduits between them, following the Purdue Model and IEC 62443 zone-and-conduit architecture. A flat network means one compromised endpoint can reach every PLC, historian, and safety system without restriction.
The most secure new-build architecture is a complete air-gap between the OT and all other networks — no conduit means no lateral movement possible
Network architecture is less important than deploying strong OT-validated endpoint protection on every device — lateral movement cannot occur if each endpoint is independently secured
debrief:
Why B: A flat OT network is a single-failure-point architecture. One compromised engineering workstation reaches every PLC, DCS, and safety system without restriction. IEC 62443 zone-and-conduit architecture segments the network by process criticality — a compromise in one zone cannot propagate to another without traversing a controlled conduit. Design it right now; retrofit costs ten times more later.

🔗 SecureOT Suite capability: Industrial Networking & Infrastructure — zone-and-conduit architecture design.

title: One Partner, End to End
scenario: You are Group CISO at Apex Petrochemicals, Kuala Lumpur. After six months of evaluating OT security options, you have shortlisted two approaches: (A) best-of-breed — separate vendors for assessment, network design, monitoring platform, and incident response; or (B) Rockwell Automation's integrated SecureOT Suite covering all four. Your CEO asks: 'Why does it matter whether we use one vendor or four?'
question: What is the most compelling argument for a single integrated OT security partner versus a best-of-breed multi-vendor approach?
hint: Think about what happens during an OT incident when your monitoring vendor, network designer, and incident responder are three different organisations that have never worked together.
opts: The single-vendor approach is primarily a cost argument — fewer contracts, fewer licence negotiations, and a single support line reduce overhead

Best-of-breed is almost always technically superior — the best monitoring platform, the best network designer, and the best incident responder, independently selected, will outperform any single vendor's integrated stack

A single integrated OT security partner — from assessment through network design, platform deployment, and incident response — means the team that designed your network is the same team that responds to an incident in it. Context, continuity, and coordination cannot be replicated across four separate vendors under time pressure.

Rockwell Automation's integrated approach is only the right choice for organisations that already use Rockwell Automation control systems — for mixed environments, best-of-breed provides better protocol coverage
debrief:
Why C: When an OT incident fires at 2AM, you don't want three vendors trying to coordinate context across contract boundaries. The team that designed your network segmentation knows exactly where the conduits are. The team that built your asset baseline knows exactly what normal looks like. SecureOT Suite — Assess → Design → Implement → Manage — keeps that knowledge in one place, available the moment you need it.

🔗 SecureOT Suite capability: End-to-end OT security — Strategic Advisory · Platform · Professional Services · Managed Services.
